children.name: Loxodonta
children.description: African elephants — the largest land animals on Earth. The African bush elephant can weigh 6,000 kg, stand 3.7 m at the shoulder, and live for 70 years. Elephants live in complex matriarchal family units led by an experienced old female whose knowledge of food and water sources, predator threats, and the ranges of other elephant groups is critical to family survival. They demonstrate tool use, mirror self-recognition, empathy, grief, and one of the most sophisticated long-distance communication systems in the animal kingdom — infrasonic rumbles below the threshold of human hearing travel through the ground up to 10 km. The African forest elephant was only formally recognised as a distinct species in 2001 through genetic analysis.

children.name: Loxodonta africana
children.description: The largest land animal. Family groups cover home ranges of hundreds of square kilometres. Poaching reduced savanna elephant populations from approximately 1.3 million in 1979 to around 415,000 today. Ivory trade bans have enabled partial recoveries in some regions.

children.name: Loxodonta cyclotis
children.description: Smaller and darker than the bush elephant with straighter, downward-pointing tusks adapted for navigating dense forest. Recognised as a full species in 2001. Critically endangered; population reduced by over 60% in a decade due to ivory poaching and habitat loss.

children.name: Elephas
children.description: Asian elephants — smaller than their African relatives with more domed foreheads, smaller ears, and a single 'finger' on the trunk tip (African elephants have two). Asian elephants have lived alongside humans for thousands of years and have been domesticated for use in forestry, transport, religious ceremonies, and warfare. The relationship is not truly domesticated — Asian elephants in captivity are tamed wild animals, not a domestic breed. Wild Asian elephants are endangered; their forest habitat across southern and southeast Asia is fragmented by agriculture, and they come into increasing conflict with human settlements as ranges contract.

children.name: Elephas maximus
children.description: Endangered — around 48,000 individuals survive across fragmented habitat from India to Borneo. Only some males grow tusks. The Borneo pygmy elephant (subspecies E. m. borneensis) may be an isolated relic of a formerly more widespread population introduced centuries ago.
